content.text: How Azure Policy Evaluates Compliance

Azure Policy evaluates compliance in two distinct modes. The synchronous mode intercepts every CREATE, UPDATE, and DELETE request at the ARM layer and applies Deny, Append, and Modify effects before the operation completes. The asynchronous background mode runs a periodic scan of all in-scope resources against all applicable policy assignments and persists the results to the Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates store. The background scan is the source of truth for the compliance dashboard — it is not the synchronous intercept.

attrs.alt: Policy compliance evaluation triggers, engine, states, and timing

content.text: The compliance state for a resource is the result of the most recent evaluation against each applicable policy assignment. A resource that was compliant yesterday can become non-compliant today if a new policy is assigned or if the resource's properties change. Conversely, a resource that is non-compliant for a specific policy assignment may still be compliant for other assignments.
Evaluation Triggers
Policy evaluation happens on five distinct triggers. Understanding when each trigger fires determines how quickly compliance state reflects the actual resource configuration.
Trigger
Latency to Compliance State Update
Scope
ARM write request (PUT/PATCH/DELETE)
Synchronous — effect fires before response
Only the resource being written
New or updated policy assignment
Within 30 minutes
All resources in the assignment scope
Background scan cycle
Up to 24 hours
All in-scope resources across all assignments
az policy state trigger-scan
5–30 minutes (not instant)
Specified scope (subscription or MG)
Resource tag/property update
Synchronous for Deny/Append; background for Audit/AINE/DINE
Only the updated resource

trigger-scan does not return immediately. It queues a scan job and returns an HTTP 200 without a body. The compliance state updates are visible in the PolicyInsights store within 5–30 minutes for small environments, and potentially longer for large ones. Do not treat it as synchronous.
Compliance States

Each resource-policy-assignment triple is assigned a compliance state. The state reflects the most recent evaluation outcome for that combination. A single resource may be Compliant for one assignment and NonCompliant for another — the dashboard aggregates these.
State
Meaning
What to do
Compliant
Resource satisfies all policy conditions
No action — maintain current configuration
NonCompliant
Resource violates one or more policy conditions
Remediate manually or via remediation task
Not Started
Policy has Disabled effect; no evaluation ran
Enable the effect if evaluation is desired
Exempt
Resource has a matching policy exemption
Review exemption validity periodically
Conflicting
Two policies with conflicting requirements apply to the same resource
Review policy logic; use stronger effect or reconcile conditions
Querying Compliance State (Azure CLI)

# Summarize compliance for all policies in a subscription
az policy state summarize \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --query 'results.resourceDetails'

# List all non-compliant resources in a management group
az policy state list \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --filter "complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --query '[].{Resource:resourceId,Policy:policyDefinitionName,Assignment:policyAssignmentName}' \
  -o table

# Check compliance state for a specific resource across all assignments
RESOURCE_ID='/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-web-01'
az policy state list \
  --resource $RESOURCE_ID \
  --query '[].{Assignment:policyAssignmentName,State:complianceState,Effect:policyDefinitionAction}' \
  -o table

# Trigger a compliance scan for a subscription
az policy state trigger-scan --subscription $SUB_ID
echo 'Scan queued. Results available in 5-30 minutes.'

# Trigger for a management group (scans all subscriptions below it)
az policy state trigger-scan --management-group mg-corp

# Get compliance state for a specific policy assignment
az policy state list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'corp-baseline'" \
  --query '[].{Resource:resourceId,State:complianceState,RefId:policyDefinitionReferenceId}' \
  -o table | head -50
The Scope-Walk: How Assignments Are Discovered

When evaluating a resource, the policy engine walks the scope hierarchy from the resource's location upward to the root management group, collecting every policy assignment that applies at each level. An assignment at the MG root applies to every resource in the tenant. An assignment at a subscription applies only to resources in that subscription. The resulting set of applicable assignments for a resource is the union of all assignments found in the scope walk.

# View all assignments that apply to a specific resource
# (includes inherited assignments from MG hierarchy)
RESOURCE_ID='/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-web-01'

az policy state list \
  --resource $RESOURCE_ID \
  --query '[].{
    Assignment:policyAssignmentName,
    Scope:policyAssignmentScope,
    State:complianceState,
    Effect:policyDefinitionAction
  }' \
  -o table

# See all policy assignments at MG scope (inherited by all subscriptions below)
az policy assignment list \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --query '[].{Name:name,DisplayName:displayName,Scope:scope}' \
  -o table

# Policy assignment with disable-scope-walk shows direct assignments only
az policy assignment list \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID \
  --disable-scope-walk true \
  --query '[].{Name:name,Scope:scope}' \
  -o table

# notScopes prevents evaluation for specific resource paths
# To check if a resource is in a notScope, review the assignment definition
az policy assignment show \
  --name corp-baseline \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --query 'notScopes' -o json
Resource Graph: Compliance Evaluation Queries

// Overall compliance state breakdown across all policies
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| summarize
    Compliant    = countif(properties.complianceState == 'Compliant'),
    NonCompliant = countif(properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'),
    Exempt       = countif(properties.complianceState == 'Exempt'),
    NotStarted   = countif(properties.complianceState == 'Not Started')
| extend Total = Compliant + NonCompliant + Exempt + NotStarted
| extend CompliancePct = round(100.0 * Compliant / (Compliant + NonCompliant), 1)

// Resources with the most NonCompliant policy violations
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'
| summarize ViolationCount = count() by resourceId
| order by ViolationCount desc
| take 20

// Policy assignments with the most non-compliant resources
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'
| summarize NonCompliantCount = count()
    by assignment = tostring(properties.policyAssignmentName)
| order by NonCompliantCount desc

// When was the last compliance scan for a specific resource?
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where resourceId =~ '/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-web-01'
| summarize LastScan = max(todatetime(properties.timestamp))
    by resourceId, assignment = tostring(properties.policyAssignmentName)
| order by LastScan desc

// Non-compliant resources created in the last 24 hours (newly deployed without compliance)
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'
| join kind=inner (
    Resources
    | where todatetime(properties.timeCreated) > ago(24h)
    | project resourceId = tolower(id), resourceType = type
) on resourceId
| project resourceId, resourceType, assignment = tostring(properties.policyAssignmentName)
| order by resourceType asc
Compliance Evaluation for AINE and DINE

For AuditIfNotExists and DeployIfNotExists effects, the compliance evaluation is a two-phase process. Phase 1 checks whether the primary resource matches the IF condition. If it does not match, the resource is Compliant (not in scope). If it does match, Phase 2 checks whether the companion resource exists and satisfies the existenceCondition. If the companion is absent or fails the condition, the primary resource is marked NonCompliant. DINE's asynchronous deployment does not change the compliance state immediately — the resource remains NonCompliant until the deployment completes and the next evaluation cycle finds the companion in place.

# Check AINE/DINE compliance state for a VM
VM_ID='/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-web-01'

az policy state list \
  --resource $VM_ID \
  --filter "policyDefinitionAction in ('auditifnotexists','deployifnotexists')" \
  --query '[].{
    Assignment:policyAssignmentName,
    State:complianceState,
    Effect:policyDefinitionAction,
    RefId:policyDefinitionReferenceId
  }' \
  -o table

# After DINE deploys a companion, compliance state still shows NonCompliant
# until the next evaluation cycle. To check the companion exists:
az vm extension list \
  --vm-name vm-web-01 \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query '[].{Name:name,Publisher:publisher,State:provisioningState}' \
  -o table

# Trigger a re-evaluation after DINE deployment completes
az policy state trigger-scan --subscription $SUB_ID

# Compliance state should update to Compliant in 5-30 minutes
# Monitor it:
az policy state list \
  --resource $VM_ID \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'deploy-law-agent'" \
  --query '[0].{State:complianceState,LastScan:timestamp}' \
  -o json
Monitoring Compliance Changes with Azure Monitor Alerts

When a resource transitions from Compliant to NonCompliant or vice versa, the policy engine emits a PolicyStateChanged event to Azure Monitor. You can create Azure Monitor alert rules that fire when this event type matches a specific policy assignment or policy definition, enabling your team to respond to compliance drift in near-real-time rather than waiting for the next dashboard review.

# Create an alert rule that fires when any resource becomes NonCompliant
# for the corp-baseline initiative assignment

# Step 1: Create an action group for notifications
az monitor action-group create \
  --name ag-policy-violations \
  --resource-group rg-monitoring \
  --short-name polvio \
  --email-receiver name=platform email=platform@contoso.com

# Step 2: Create an activity log alert for policy state changes
az monitor activity-log alert create \
  --name alert-policy-noncompliant \
  --resource-group rg-monitoring \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --condition category=Policy and level=Warning \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID \
  --action-group /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-monitoring/providers/microsoft.insights/actionGroups/ag-policy-violations

# Step 3: For finer-grained alerting, use Azure Monitor Metric Alerts
# with the PolicyInsights resource as the target
az monitor metrics alert create \
  --name alert-policy-violations-critical \
  --resource-group rg-monitoring \
  --scopes /subscriptions/$SUB_ID \
  --condition "count 'PolicyInsights' 'NonCompliantResources' > 0" \
  --description 'Alert when any resource becomes NonCompliant' \
  --evaluation-frequency 5m \
  --window-size 15m \
  --action /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-monitoring/providers/microsoft.insights/actionGroups/ag-policy-violations

# View recent PolicyStateChanged events in the Activity Log
az monitor activity-log list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --resource-provider Microsoft.PolicyInsights \
  --start-time $(date -u -d '24 hours ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ 2>/dev/null || date -u -v-24H +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) \
  --query '[].{Time:eventTimestamp,Op:operationName.value,Status:status.value,Resource:resourceId}' \
  -o table
Compliance Evaluation for Exempt Resources

Policy exemptions affect compliance calculation differently depending on the exemption category. A Waiver exemption acknowledges that a control is intentionally bypassed — the resource is considered Exempt rather than NonCompliant. A Mitigated exemption signals that alternative controls satisfy the policy requirement. Both categories remove the resource from the NonCompliant count in the compliance percentage formula, improving the reported score. Exemptions do not prevent the policy engine from evaluating the resource — evaluation still runs, but the result is overridden to Exempt.

# Verify exemption is applying correctly to a specific resource
RESOURCE_ID='/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-legacy/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-legacy-01'

# Check that exemption exists and targets the correct assignment
az policy exemption list \
  --scope $RESOURCE_ID \
  --query '[].{Name:name,Category:exemptionCategory,Assignment:policyAssignmentId,Expiry:expiresOn}' \
  -o table

# Check compliance state — should show Exempt for the exempted assignment
az policy state list \
  --resource $RESOURCE_ID \
  --query '[].{Assignment:policyAssignmentName,State:complianceState}' \
  -o table

# List all Exempt resources across the management group
az policy state list \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --filter "complianceState eq 'Exempt'" \
  --query '[].{Resource:resourceId,Assignment:policyAssignmentName,Group:resourceGroup,Sub:subscriptionId}' \
  -o table

# Resource Graph: find exemptions expiring in the next 14 days
az graph query -q "
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.Authorization/policyExemptions'
| where isnotempty(properties.expiresOn)
| where todatetime(properties.expiresOn) between (now() .. now() + 14d)
| project name, scope = id, expiresOn = todatetime(properties.expiresOn), category = tostring(properties.exemptionCategory)
| order by expiresOn asc
" --management-groups mg-corp
Policy Evaluation Modes: All vs Indexed

Every policy definition has a mode field that controls which resource types are evaluated. Mode Indexed restricts evaluation to resource types that support tags and location (the common ARM resource types like VMs, storage accounts, and key vaults). Mode All evaluates all resource types including subscription-level resources, resource groups, and Management Group resources. The wrong mode can cause a policy to silently skip the resources it should be evaluating.
Mode
Evaluates
Use When
Indexed
Resources that support tags and location (compute, storage, network, etc.)
Tag enforcement, location restriction, property checks on common resource types
All
All resources including subscriptions, resource groups, MG resources, and non-standard types
Subscription-level controls (security contacts, defender plans), resource group policies, any type that may not support tags
Common Evaluation Delays and Troubleshooting
Symptom
Likely Cause
Resolution
Resource deployed but still NonCompliant 30+ minutes later
DINE deployment hasn't completed or companion scan not yet run
Check remediation task status; run trigger-scan; verify extension/companion exists
Compliance dashboard shows old data
Background scan runs every 24h; dashboard may be stale
Run az policy state trigger-scan; allow 5–30 min; refresh dashboard
New policy assignment shows 'Not Started' for all resources
Effect=Disabled or policy evaluation hasn't run yet
Trigger a scan; verify effect parameter is not Disabled
Resource is Compliant but appears misconfigured
existenceCondition is too permissive; Compliant because ANY companion exists
Tighten existenceCondition to check specific properties (workspace ID, state, publisher)
Policy state list returns no results for a resource
Resource is in a notScopes exclusion or subscription is not in assignment scope
Check assignment notScopes; verify subscription is in MG tree under assignment scope
trigger-scan completes but results don't update
Large MG scope — scan is still in progress; results partial
Wait longer; check at subscription scope instead of MG scope
Summary

Azure Policy compliance evaluation runs both synchronously (at ARM request time for blocking effects) and asynchronously (background scan every 24h and on-demand via trigger-scan). The policy engine walks the management group hierarchy to collect all applicable assignments, evaluates each resource against the IF condition and any existence checks, and persists the result to Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates. Compliance state for AINE and DINE resources reflects the most recent scan outcome, not the real-time deployment state — trigger a scan after companion resource deployment to see the updated state.
